skill.name: pyopenms-skill
skill.description: Comprehensive tool for computational mass spectrometry using PyOpenMS; use when you need to read/write MS formats (mzML/mzXML/MGF), run signal processing (smoothing/peak picking), detect isotope features, or perform peptide identification in proteomics/metabolomics workflows.
skill.summary: PyOpenMS skill for reading mass spectrometry files, processing spectra, and detecting features in proteomics and metabolomics data.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 30 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 7 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags refer to signal processing algorithms (GaussFilter, PeakPicker) in documentation, not cryptographic functions. The 21 'shell backtick execution' flags are markdown code block delimiters (```python, ```bash) and inline code references, not Ruby shell commands. The 2 network and reconnaissance flags reference a legitimate source repository URL and Python function call in example code. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or other malicious patterns were detected. The skill is a legitimate scientific computing tool for mass spectrometry data analysis.

medium_findings.title: Markdown code blocks flagged as shell backtick execution
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: 21 instances of backticks flagged as Ruby shell execution. All instances are markdown code block delimiters (```python, ```bash) or inline code formatting in documentation files. No actual shell command execution occurs.

medium_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm pattern - false positive
medium_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Scanner flagged the word 'algorithm' in scientific documentation as a weak cryptographic algorithm. The referenced algorithms are signal processing algorithms (GaussFilter for smoothing, PeakPickerHiRes for peak detection) used in computational mass spectrometry, not cryptographic functions.

low_findings.title: Source repository URL flagged as hardcoded URL
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The URL at SKILL.md:7 is a legitimate source repository attribution link pointing to the skill's GitHub repository. This is standard practice for open-source skill documentation.

low_findings.title: Legacy audit text flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Text in pyopenms-skill_audit_result_v1.json:23 is a legacy audit finding discussing scientific integrity. No reconnaissance content is present.

low_findings.title: Python function call flagged as network reconnaissance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: SKILL.md:48 contains 'run_workflow("data.mzML", apply_filter=True)' which is a Python function call in example code for processing mass spectrometry files. No network activity is involved.

content.user_title: Analyze Mass Spectrometry Data with PyOpenMS
content.value_statement: Researchers need to process complex mass spectrometry data but lack an easy way to integrate OpenMS algorithms with Python workflows. This skill provides ready-to-use PyOpenMS scripts for reading MS file formats, applying signal processing filters, and detecting features for proteomics and metabolomics analysis.
actual_capabilities: Read and write mass spectrometry file formats including mzML, mzXML, and MGF
Apply signal processing filters such as Gaussian smoothing, baseline correction, and peak picking
Detect isotope patterns and link features for quantitative proteomics
Execute a complete Load-Process-Analyze workflow using the provided process_ms.py script
Integrate peptide and protein identification pipelines via OpenMS-compatible components
limitations: Requires PyOpenMS and its C++ dependencies, which may not be pre-installed in all environments
Designed for standard MS file formats; custom vendor formats may need prior conversion
Signal processing parameters (e.g., Gaussian width) must be configured per experiment and may need tuning
Does not include visualization or plotting utilities for processed spectra

use_cases.title: Batch Process Mass Spectrometry Files
use_cases.description: A lab technician needs to convert and validate a batch of raw mzML files before downstream statistical analysis. The skill reads multiple formats and applies consistent preprocessing.
use_cases.target_user: Lab technician or data analyst handling MS file batches

use_cases.title: Clean and Denoise Spectra for Publication
use_cases.description: A researcher needs to apply Gaussian smoothing and baseline correction to raw spectra to produce publication-ready data. The skill's signal processing pipeline handles these steps.
use_cases.target_user: Research scientist preparing spectral data for publication

use_cases.title: Detect Isotope Features in Metabolomics Data
use_cases.description: A metabolomics researcher wants to identify and link isotope patterns across spectra for quantification. The skill provides feature detection algorithms through PyOpenMS.

prompt_templates.title: Basic MS File Reading
prompt_templates.prompt: Read an mzML file using the PyOpenMS skill and print the number of spectra and first spectrum peak count.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user loading data to verify the file is valid

prompt_templates.title: Apply Signal Smoothing to Spectra
prompt_templates.prompt: Load an mzML file, apply Gaussian smoothing with a width of 0.5, then compare the number of peaks before and after smoothing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Biologist cleaning noisy spectra before downstream analysis

prompt_templates.title: Full Feature Detection Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the complete Load-Process-Analyze workflow on a set of mzML files. For each file, report the spectrum count, mz range, and number of detected features.
prompt_templates.scenario: Proteomics researcher processing multiple samples for quantification

prompt_templates.title: Custom Batch Processing Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a batch processing script that reads all mzML files in a directory, applies peak picking and isotope detection to each, and produces a combined feature table with file source annotations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user building an automated analysis pipeline for a multi-sample experiment

output_examples.input: Read an mzML file and return spectrum statistics
output_examples.output: The skill loads the file, counts spectra, and reports: 1245 spectra found, first spectrum contains 342 peaks with mz range 100.2 to 1500.8

output_examples.input: Apply Gaussian smoothing to reduce noise in a spectrum
output_examples.output: The skill processes the raw data: original spectrum had 892 peaks with visible noise, after smoothing with width 0.3 the noise floor is reduced and 487 significant peaks remain for downstream analysis

best_practices: Always verify that PyOpenMS and its dependencies are installed before running workflow scripts
Test signal processing parameters on a single representative file before applying to a full batch
Use the provided process_ms.py script as a template and customize filter parameters to match your instrument and experiment type
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill with unsupported proprietary MS vendor formats without first converting them to mzML or mzXML
Do not apply the same smoothing parameters to all data types without testing, as optimal values vary by instrument and sample type
Do not skip the input file validation step, as missing or corrupted files will cause workflow failures midway through processing

faq.question: What file formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports standard open MS formats: mzML, mzXML, and MGF. Proprietary vendor formats may need prior conversion.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything before using this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you need to install PyOpenMS, pandas, and numpy. Use the command: uv pip install pyopenms pandas numpy

faq.question: Can I use this skill for metabolomics data?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill supports both proteomics and metabolomics workflows. Feature detection and isotope pattern analysis work for both domains.

faq.question: How do I adjust the smoothing filter strength?
faq.answer: Modify the gaussian_width parameter in the GaussFilter configuration. A smaller value preserves more detail, while a larger value removes more noise.

faq.question: Does this skill work with large MS files?
faq.answer: PyOpenMS handles large files efficiently through its C++ backend. Performance depends on available memory and the number of spectra in the file.

faq.question: Can I add custom processing steps to the workflow?
faq.answer: Yes, the process_ms.py script is designed as a template. You can add additional PyOpenMS filters or analysis steps by modifying the run_workflow function.
